    Development environment setup

    📄

    Setting up your development environment.

    Lesson 1

    In this short series of 4 videos we will set up a development environment on which we will expand in the upcoming 8 weeks.


    You can find the relevant code here: https://github.com/swirl-ai/ai-engineering-bootcamp-prerequisites. If you are not an experienced programmer we highly suggest coding along the videos instead of just cloning the repository.


    You can also follow a written version of Python development environment setup available in the section after the videos.


    Video 1:


    • Setting up development environment.

    • Configuring your LLM API Keys.

    • Testing your API Keys.




    Video 2:


    • Building a simple Streamlit application.

    • Dockerising the application.

    • Deploying the application via Docker Compose.




    Video 3:


    • Extracting LLM calling logic into a FastAPI REST Server.

    • Deploying the server as a separate service via Docker Compose.




    Video 4:


    • Rewriting Streamlit Frontend application to use previously deployed FastAPI server.

    • Deploying FastAPI and Streamlit applications as separate container via Docker Compose.




    Step-by-Step Guide for Python environment setup (optional if you followed Video 1 and 2)



    Step 1: Python Installation


    Ensure you have Python 3.10 or later installed. Check your version.

    python --version



    Step 2: Dependency Management


    We are going to use uv package manager in our projects. Install it by running:

    pip install uv



    Step 3: Virtual Environment


    Using virtual environments helps isolate dependencies for each project. Create and activate a virtual environment using uv:

    uv venv .venv --python 3.12
source .venv/bin/activate


    Step 4: IDE and Tools


    Select an IDE optimized for Python:


    • Recommended: Cursor.

    • Alternative: PyCharm or Visual Studio Code with Python and GitHub Copilot extensions.


    While you can run Notebooks directly in the suggested IDEs, you can also opt for a dedicated Notebook IDE, e.g.:


    • Jupyter Lab.

    • Google Colab.


    Step 5: Version Control


    Use Git to manage your project's codebase:


    git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial project setup"


    Alternatively, you can first create a GitHub repository and then pull it directly via a chosen IDE.



    Step 6: GitHub Repository Setup


    Setting up a remote repository allows collaboration and backup of your project:


    • Create a new repository on GitHub.

    • Link your local repository to the remote one:


    git remote add origin <repository_url>
git branch -M main
git push -u origin main


    Step 7: Containerisation with Docker


    Containerising applications ensures consistent deployment environments:


    • Install Docker Desktop:

      • Docker Desktop for Windows/Mac

      • Docker Desktop for Linux
